verses.commentary: Accept the one whose faith is weak, without quarreling over disputable matters. Weak faith (astheneō, ἀσθενέω) refers to those with scruples over non-essential matters. Accept (proslambanō, προσλαμβάνω) means welcome, receive. Without quarreling over disputable matters: avoid debating opinions. The strong must welcome the weak, not judge them.

One person's faith allows them to eat anything, but another, whose faith is weak, eats only vegetables. The issue: dietary restrictions. The strong eat anything (no dietary laws). The weak eat only vegetables (to avoid meat offered to idols or ceremonially unclean meat). Faith here means conviction, not saving faith. Dietary liberty is a matter of conscience, not doctrine.

The one who eats everything must not treat with contempt the one who does not, and the one who does not eat everything must not judge the one who does, for God has accepted them. Mutual command: strong must not despise (exoutheneō, ἐξουθενέω) the weak; weak must not judge (krinō, κρίνω) the strong. The ground: God has accepted both. Divine acceptance trumps human judgment.

Who are you to judge someone else's servant? To their own master, servants stand or fall. And they will stand, for the Lord is able to make them stand. Judging another's servant is overstepping. Each believer is accountable to their own Master (Christ). Standing or falling is determined by the Lord, not fellow believers. The Lord is able to make them stand affirms grace's sufficiency.

One person considers one day more sacred than another; another considers every day alike. Each of them should be fully convinced in their own mind. The issue: observance of special days (likely Jewish Sabbaths or festivals). Some regard certain days as sacred; others treat all days alike. Each should be fully convinced (plērophoreō, πληροφορέω) in their own mind. Conviction matters more than uniformity in non-essentials.

Whoever regards one day as special does so to the Lord. Whoever eats meat does so to the Lord, for they give thanks to God; and whoever abstains does so to the Lord and gives thanks to God. The key: motivation. Observing days or eating/abstaining can all be done to the Lord. Thanksgiving to God sanctifies the action. Motive, not the action itself, determines acceptability. Both practices honor God if done in faith.

For none of us lives for ourselves alone, and none of us dies for ourselves alone. We do not live or die in isolation. Our lives belong to the Lord, not ourselves. Independence is a myth. We are part of a body, accountable to God and connected to others.

If we live, we live for the Lord; and if we die, we die for the Lord. So, whether we live or die, we belong to the Lord. Life and death are both for the Lord. Belonging (kyriou, κυρίου) emphasizes ownership. Christ is Lord of life and death. This is comprehensive lordship.

For this very reason, Christ died and returned to life so that He might be the Lord of both the dead and the living. Christ's death and resurrection established His lordship over all. Dead and living encompasses everyone. His resurrection qualifies Him to judge and rule. Universal lordship is the result of His redemptive work.

You, then, why do you judge your brother or sister? Or why do you treat them with contempt? For we will all stand before God's judgment seat. Two questions: why judge? Why despise? Both are condemned. The reason: we all face God's judgment seat (bēma, βῆμα, tribunal). Since God judges, we must not. Our accountability is to Him, not each other.

It is written: As surely as I live, says the Lord, every knee will bow before me; every tongue will acknowledge God (Isaiah 45:23). Universal acknowledgment of God is certain. Every knee will bow (future, not hypothetical). Every tongue will acknowledge (confess). This is eschatological: all will submit to God's authority. Since God is the final Judge, human judgment is presumptuous.

So then, each of us will give an account of ourselves to God. Individual accountability. Each of us (not others) will give account to God. This focuses responsibility on self, not judging others. We answer for ourselves, not for others.

Therefore let us stop passing judgment on one another. Instead, make up your mind not to put any stumbling block or obstacle in the way of a brother or sister. Stop judging each other. Instead, focus on not causing others to stumble. Stumbling block (proskomma, πρόσκομμα) is an obstacle that trips. Obstacle (skandalon, σκάνδαλον) is a trap. Love avoids causing others to fall.

I am convinced, being fully persuaded in the Lord Jesus, that nothing is unclean in itself. But if anyone regards something as unclean, then for that person it is unclean. Paul's conviction: nothing is unclean (koinos, κοινός, common, ceremonially impure) in itself. This is objective truth (echoing Mark 7:19). But subjective conscience matters: if someone regards it unclean, for them it is. Conscience binds even when mistaken. Violating conscience is sin, even in neutral matters.

If your brother or sister is distressed because of what you eat, you are no longer acting in love. Do not by your eating destroy someone for whom Christ died. Distressing (lypeō, λυπέω, grieving) a weaker brother violates love. Do not destroy (apollymi, ἀπόλλυμι) means do not ruin or cause to stumble. The worth of the brother: Christ died for him. Liberty must yield to love. Christ's sacrifice should constrain our freedom.

Therefore do not let what you know is good be spoken of as evil. What you know is good (your liberty) must not be slandered (blasphēmeō, βλασφημέω). Insisting on liberty can cause others to speak evil of the gospel. Guarding the gospel's reputation matters more than exercising freedom.

For the kingdom of God is not a matter of eating and drinking, but of righteousness, peace and joy in the Holy Spirit. The kingdom's essence is not external rules (eating and drinking) but internal realities: righteousness (right standing with God), peace (shalom, wholeness), and joy in the Holy Spirit. These matter infinitely more than dietary practices. Majoring on minors misses the kingdom.

Because anyone who serves Christ in this way is pleasing to God and receives human approval. Serving Christ in this way means prioritizing righteousness, peace, and joy over disputable matters. This pleases God and gains human approval (dokimos, δόκιμος, approved). Christlike love wins both divine and human commendation.

Let us therefore make every effort to do what leads to peace and to mutual edification. A positive command: pursue (diōkō, διώκω, actively chase) peace and mutual edification (oikodomē, οἰκοδομή, building up). Peace and edification are goals worth pursuing. This requires effort and intentionality.

Do not destroy the work of God for the sake of food. All food is clean, but it is wrong for a person to eat anything that causes someone else to stumble. Do not destroy (katalyō, καταλύω, tear down) God's work (a person) over food. All food is clean (affirms liberty). But eating in a way that causes stumbling is evil (kakon, κακόν). Liberty yielded for love's sake honors God.

It is better not to eat meat or drink wine or to do anything else that will cause your brother or sister to fall. Better means noble, honorable (kalon, καλόν). Abstaining from meat or wine (even though permissible) to avoid causing another to fall is virtuous. Love limits liberty. Christian freedom is constrained by love for others.

So whatever you believe about these things keep between yourself and God. Blessed is the one who does not condemn himself by what he approves. Personal convictions about disputable matters should be kept private. Do not flaunt liberty. Blessed (makarios, μακάριος, happy) is the one who does not condemn himself. If conscience is clear (you do not violate your own conviction), you are blessed. Self-condemnation brings misery.

But whoever has doubts is condemned if they eat, because their eating is not from faith; and everything that does not come from faith is sin. Acting against conscience (with doubts) brings condemnation (self-judgment). Eating not from faith means acting without conviction. Everything not from faith is sin. This does not mean all actions require saving faith, but all actions must align with conviction. Violating conscience is always sin, even in neutral matters.
